text.title: Quality Attribute Trade-offs and SLO Framework
text.summary: Common QA trade-off pairs and the 6-part scenario format for expressing measurable quality requirements; SLI/SLO/SLA framework with error budget calculation.

rule.statement: Quality attributes conflict. Every architecture decision improves some attributes at the cost of others. Make trade-offs explicit and document them in the ADR. Never claim an architecture improves all attributes simultaneously.
rule.rationale: Implicit trade-offs lead to architecture that optimizes for the last concern raised in a meeting. Explicit trade-offs force prioritization and give future architects the reasoning for constraints.

list.item: Performance vs Security: encryption, authorization checks, and input validation all add latency
Scalability vs Consistency: horizontal scale requires relaxing strong consistency (CAP theorem: choose AP or CP)
Availability vs Consistency: multi-region active/active requires eventual consistency; strong consistency requires quorum and sacrifices availability during partition
Maintainability vs Performance: caching improves performance but adds staleness complexity; async processing improves throughput but complicates error handling and observability
Security vs Usability: additional auth factors reduce the attack surface but increase friction
Testability vs Performance: in-process abstractions (Repository, ports) enable fast unit tests but add one indirection layer compared to direct database calls

rule.statement: Express every quality requirement as a 6-part scenario: Source, Stimulus, Environment, Artifact, Response, Response Measure. A requirement without a Response Measure cannot be validated in architecture review or acceptance testing.
rule.rationale: Prose quality requirements ("the system should be highly available") are unverifiable. The 6-part format forces concreteness: who triggers the condition, what the system does, and what specific numeric threshold defines success.

list.item: Source: who or what triggers the quality scenario — a user, an external service, an attacker, a spike in traffic
Stimulus: the event that triggers the scenario — a request, a failure, a spike, an attack
Environment: the system's state when the stimulus occurs — normal operation, overloaded, degraded, maintenance
Artifact: which part of the system is affected — the entire system, a service, a database, a message queue
Response: what the system does — processes, rejects, queues, degrades gracefully
Response Measure: the testable threshold — p99 <300ms, availability >99.9%, recovery <15 min

example.code: Source: End user Stimulus: Submits a product search query Environment: Normal operation, 500 concurrent users Artifact: Product search API endpoint Response: Returns search results Response Measure: p95 latency < 200ms; p99 < 500ms; 0% error rate

rule.statement: Define SLIs (what you measure), SLOs (the target threshold), and error budgets (allowed failure). Alert when error budget burn rate is high, not when individual metrics breach thresholds.
rule.rationale: Alerting on individual metric thresholds produces alert fatigue and misses the real question: is the user experience degraded right now? Error budget burn rate answers that question.

list.item: SLI (Service Level Indicator): the measured metric — request success rate, p99 latency, availability
SLO (Service Level Objective): the target threshold — 99.9% success rate, p99 <500ms over 30 days
SLA (Service Level Agreement): contractual commitment to customers — subset of SLOs with financial consequences
Error budget: 100% - SLO target; a 99.9% SLO has 0.1% error budget = 43.8 minutes/month of allowed failures
Burn rate: how fast the error budget is being consumed; >14x burn rate = budget exhausted in <1 hour → page
